data.title: Ogboni Chief's Textile

thumbnail.alt_text: A work made of cotton, indigo dyed; sixteen strips of plain weave, joined by hand stitching; embroidered with wool in chain stitches; edged with cotton, buttonhole stitched uncut fringe.

data.description: An elder of the Ogboni council—a sociopolitical society of the Iwo Yoruba in Nigeria—would have worn this elaborately embroidered textile during important ceremonies and moments of significant transition, like the coronation or burial of a king. The Yoruba believe in the sacred unity of three cosmic spheres, expressed in the three bands of designs that decorate the garment. The birds symbolize the ability to hover above and move between spiritual and earthly realms. In this composition they also represent Ìyàmi, women who wield spiritual powers of elemental creation, destruction, and healing.
data.dimensions: 162.5 × 275.6 cm (64 × 108 1/2 in.); Approximate strip: W.: 10 cm (4 in.)
data.medium_display: Cotton, indigo dyed; sixteen strips of plain weave, joined by hand stitching; embroidered with wool in chain stitches; edged with cotton, buttonhole stitched uncut fringe

data.title: Rainbow People Have Arrived (Nááts’íílid Bee Yikáh)

thumbnail.alt_text: A work made of wool, dovetail and single interlocking tapestry weave; edges finished with three strand weft twining with four knotted corner tassels.

data.artist_display: Hastiin Tła (Left-Handed Man; also known as Hosteen Klah), (Diné (Navajo), 1867–1937)
data.place_of_origin: Navajo Nation, Arizona, New Mexico, & Utah
data.description:
Hastiin Tła was a Medicine Man specializing in healing chants. In this tapestry based on a sand painting, he depicted the last morning of the nine-day Nightway Chant for healing, although he may have intentionally omitted a symbolic item included in the original painting. The four pairs of Rainbow People are guardians that keep the patient safe. Each consists of a female with a square head and male with a round head, both holding spruce branches and feathers. They stand atop a stone in one of the Diné’s sacred, ceremonial colors: shell-white for the east, turquoise-blue for the south, abalone-yellow for the west, and jet-black for the north. Two Holy People and their spirits trail across the sky, indicating that the healing is done.
—Lynda Teller Pete, fifth-generation Diné tapestry weaver
data.medium_display: Wool, dovetail and single interlocking tapestry weave; edges finished with three strand weft twining with four knotted corner tassels
data.publication_history: Richard Townsend with Elizabeth Pope, Indian Art of the Americas at the Art Institute of Chicago (Chicago: Art Institute of Chicago, 2016), 90, cat. 67. Lynda Teller Pete and Barbara Teller Ornelas, "The Enduring Beauty of Diné Textiles," in On Loss and Absence: Textiles of Mourning and Survival, eds. Isaac Facio, Nneka Kai, L Vinebaum, and Anne Wilson, exh. cat. (Chicago: Art Institute of Chicago, 2025), 191, fig. 5 (ill.).
data.exhibition_history: Art Institute of Chicago, African and Amerindian Art, Gallery 136, Apr. 17-Aug. 11, 2015. Art Institute of Chicago, Elizabeth F. Cheney and Agnes Allerton Textile Galleries, On Loss and Absence: Textiles of Mourning and Survival, Sept. 6, 2025–Mar. 15, 2026, cat.
data.provenance_text: The artist [this and the following according letter from Philip Cohen, Sherwoods Gallery; June 21, 2002; in curatorial object file]; sold to Mrs. Harold S. Gladwin (died 1983), Globe, AZ, then Santa Barbara, CA, 1920s/1930s; given to the Museum of Northern Arizona, Flagstaff, AZ, 1958; deaccessioned, May 2002; sold through Steven Diamant, Direct Art/Fine Arts of the Southwest, Santa Fe, NM [The Arizona Republic, Apr. 26, 2005]. Philip Cohen, Sherwoods Sprit of America Gallery, Santa Fe, NM, by July 22, 2002 [Invoice S 2798, item SF1034,  July 22, 2002; incoming permanent receipt, RX23745, Aug. 5, 2002; copies in curatorial object file]; sold to the Art Institute of Chicago, 2002.

data.title: Ceremonial Hanging (Palepai)

thumbnail.alt_text: Large rectangle of fabric, geometric shapes depicting ship and people, in red, blue and yellow.

data.artist_display:
Paminggir people
Lampung area, South Sumatra, Indonesia
data.description: The Paminggir aristocracy employed a type of ritual hanging commonly known as a ship cloth, a name that refers to one of the principal motifs of such textiles. These hangings display a very limited repertoire of patterns, including one or two red ships, a single blue ship, tree images, buildings, and rows of stylized anthropomorphic figures. The indigenous name for textiles of this type, palepai, probably goes back to the old Javanese word lepih, which means “to fold” or “to double.” Palepai served as markers of status and perhaps even denoted descent. Indeed, title, claim to leadership, and certain other privileges accompanied the possession of palepai. These textiles were also important requisites during rites of passage, such as marriage, death, or accession to a higher rank—events that were thought to be unstable moments fraught with danger. The ship images that appear on this palepai would have indicated an appropriate agent of safe conduct from one stage of life to another. The red vessels are also understood to represent the upper world, the abode of the ancestors, which can be reached by the ship of death. Since the Lampung economy was based on sea trade, ships served as symbols of wealth and power as well.
data.medium_display: Cotton, silk, and silver-leaf-over-lacquered-paper-strip-wrapped bast fiber (probably ramie), plain weave with supplementary patterning and brocading wefts, main warp fringe
data.credit_line: Gift of E. M. Bakwin Indonesian Textile Collection
data.publication_history:
Brigitte Kahn Majlis, "Behind the Warps and Wefts: Indonesian Textiles in Context," The Art of Indonesian Textiles: The E. M. Bakwin Collection at the Art Institute of Chicago, Art Institute of Chicago Museum Studies, 33 no. 2 (2007): 33-34, cat. 5.

Folayemi Wilson, "Of Water and Sky Along a Fibered Path," in On Loss and Absence: Textiles of Mourning and Survival, eds. Isaac Facio, Nneka Kai, L Vinebaum, and Anne Wilson, exh. cat. (Chicago: Art Institute of Chicago, 2025), 74-75, fig. 5 (ill.).
data.exhibition_history: Art Institute of Chicago, Galleries 108-109, The Art of Indonesian Textiles: The E. M. Bakwin Collection at the Art Institute of Chicago, Dec. 1, 2007–Mar. 2, 2008, cat. Art Institute of Chicago, Elizabeth F. Cheney and Agnes Allerton Textile Galleries, On Loss and Absence: Textiles of Mourning and Survival, Sept. 6, 2025–Mar. 15, 2026, cat.

data.title: Stoke-by-Nayland

thumbnail.alt_text: Large English country landscape painting, farmers with horses, green trees.

data.description:
“What say you to a summer morning?” John Constable wrote of this painting in a letter to a friend. Even after many years living in London, Constable continued to portray the countryside, dear to him from boyhood. Stoke-by-Nayland lies a few miles from his native village of East Bergholt in Suffolk. In this view, he divided the canvas between a brilliant, airy vista toward the hamlet on the left and a shady, tunnel-like country lane leading off to the right. Constable explained in his letter that the painting depicted a speciﬁc time, a morning in “July or August, at eight or nine o’clock, after a slight shower during the night, to enhance the dews in the shadowed part of the picture.” The artist emphasized the abundance of water through his painting technique, ﬂecking the surface with white highlights to create an effect of sparkling wetness. Here, the whole scene appears dewy, with a stream and puddles in the foreground and a central tree that droops from the weight of rainwater, emphasizing the fertile land.

Painted as much with a palette knife as with brushes, Stoke-by-Nayland lacks the ﬁnish of pictures Constable exhibited publicly; it was meant as a full-scale sketch for a work that he never realized. Nonetheless, this canvas seems to capture Constable’s delight in freely scribbling and scraping the image into existence—what it lacks in detail it gains in atmosphere. The roughness of the surface evokes the textures of real landscape, and the coexistence of natural and built elements in the scene embodies an ideal of harmony indicative of Constable’s vision of rural England.
data.short_description: John Constable produced this full-size oil sketch for an unrealized painting near the end of his life. Its subject—the countryside of Constable's youth in Suffolk, England—was one of the artist's favorites. To achieve the effect of sparkling wetness and fresh earth and air, Constable painted with a palette knife, flecking the surface with white highlights as he sketched and scraped the picture into existence.
data.credit_line: Mr. and Mrs. W. W. Kimball Collection

data.title: Skyphos (Drinking Cup)

thumbnail.alt_text: A wide-mouthed drinking cup glazed in black with two handles extending from opposite sides.

data.description: During the course of the 5th and 4th centuries BCE, black vessels (commonly called black-glaze vessels) were made with increasing frequency in both Greece and South Italy. Many of them replicate the shape of metal vessels. Others have detailing that is molded or incised. While the quality of these vessels varies greatly, all would have been less expensive than vessels decorated in other contemporary techniques, for example, in red-figure.
data.credit_line: Gift of Philip D. Armour and Charles L. Hutchinson
data.publication_history:
W.M.R. French, 1889-?. Notes [on a] journey to Europe with Mr. and Mrs. C.L. Hutchinson starting from New York Sat'y, 1889-?, (unpublished manuscript in Art Institute of Chicago Archives), p. 26.

data.title: Still Life with Queen

thumbnail.alt_text: A work made of oil on canvas.

data.publication_history:
Art Institute of Chicago, The Arthur Jerome Eddy Collection of Modern Paintings and Sculpture, exh. cat. (Chicago: The Art Institute of Chicago, 1931), 22, cat. 13.

Art Institute of Chicago, A Century of Progress Exhibition of Paintings and Sculpture, exh. cat. (Chicago: The Art Institute of Chicago, 1933), 82, cat. 761.

Peter Selz, German Expressionist Painting (Berkeley: University of California Press, 1957), 209, pl. 89.

Art Institute of Chicago, Paintings in the Art Institute of Chicago: A Catalogue of the Picture Collection (Chicago: Art Institute of Chicago, 1961), 342.

The German Expressionists and Their Contemporaries: 50 Years of Fantasy and Frenzy, exh. cat. (South Bend, IN.: University of Notre Dame, Art Gallery, 1969), n.p., cat. 84a.

German and Austrian Expressionism: Art in a Turbulent Era, exh. cat. (Chicago: Museum of Contemporary Art, 1978), 29, no cat. no.

Expressionism and Its Legacy: German Art from 1905 to the Present: The Frankfurter Kunstkabinett Hannah Bekker vom Rath, exh. cat. (St. Petersburg, FL.: Museum of Fine Arts, 1980), n.p., cat. 9.

Anne Mochon, Gabriele Münter, between Munich and Murnau, exh. cat. (Cambridge, MA.: Busch-Reisinger Museum, Harvard University, 1980), 51, cat. 47 (color ill.).

Annegret Hoberg, Gabriele Münter, 1877–1962: Retrospektive, exh. cat. (München: Prestel, 1992), 40, 65, cat. 118 (color ill.).

James Wood, Treasures of 19th- and 20th-Century Painting, the Art Institute of Chicago (New York: Abbeville Press, 1993), 207 (color ill.).

Reinhold Heller, Gabriele Münter: The Years of Expressionism, 1903-1920, exh. cat. (Munich and New York: Prestel, 1997), 109, cat. 65 (color ill.).

Annegret Hoberg, Gabriele Münter (Munich and New York: Prestel, 2003), 28, 29, 76, pl. 42 (color ill.), as Stilleben mit Königin.

Vivian Endicott Barnett, The Chicago Lawyer Arthur Jerome Eddy and His Eclectic Art Collection (Philadelphia: American Philosophical Society Press, 2022), 109, 111, fig. 12.2 (color ill.).

Elizabeth Siegel, The Bradley Collection of Modern Art, exh. cat. (Milwaukee: Milwaukee Art Museum, 2025), 32 (color ill.).

Victoria Horrocks, Isabelle Jansen, Iris Müller-Westermann, Dorothy Price, and Megan M. Fontanella, Gabriele Münter: Contours of a World, exh. cat. (New York: Guggenheim Museum, 2025, 123 (color ill.), 163.
data.exhibition_history:
Munich, Der neue Kunstsalon Dietzel, G. Münter, Kollektiv-Ausstellung, March–April 1913 (traveled to Dresden, Frankfurt, and Stuttgart), cat. 21.

Art Institute of Chicago, The Arthur Jerome Eddy Collection of Modern Paintings and Sculpture, Dec. 22, 1931–Jan. 17, 1932, cat. 13.

Art Institute of Chicago, A Century of Progress Exhibition of Paintings and Sculpture, June 1–Nov. 1, 1933, cat. 761.

South Bend, IN, Art Gallery, University of Notre Dame, The German Expressionists and Their Contemporaries: 50 Years of Fantasy and Frenzy, Dec. 7, 1969–Feb. 15, 1970, cat. 84a.

Chicago, Museum of Contemporary Art, German and Austrian Expressionism: Art in a Turbulent Era, Mar. 10–Apr. 30, 1978, no cat. no.

St. Petersburg, FL, Museum of Fine Arts, Expressionism and Its Legacy: German Art from 1905 to the Present: The Frankfurter Kunstkabinett Hannah Bekker vom Rath, Jan. 19–Feb. 24, 1980, cat. 9.

Cambridge, MA, Harvard University, Busch-Reisinger Museum, Gabriele Münter, between Munich and Murnau, Sept. 25–Nov. 8, 1980, cat. 47; Princeton, NJ, Princeton University Art Museum, Nov. 22, 1980–Jan. 18, 1981.

Munich, Städtischen Galerie im Lenbachhaus, Gabriele Münter, 1877–1962: Retrospektive, July 29–Nov. 1, 1992, cat. 118; Frankfurt, Schirn Kunsthalle, Nov. 29–Feb. 10, 1993; Stockholm, Liljevalchs Konsthall, Apr. 4–May 31, 1993.

Milwaukee, Milwaukee Art Museum, Gabriele Münter: The Years of Expressionism, 1903-1920, Dec. 5, 1997–Mar. 1, 1998, aat. 65; Columbus OH, Columbus Museum of Art, Apr. 18–June 21, 1998; Richmond, VA, Virginia Museum of Fine Arts, July 31–Sept. 21, 1998; San Antonio, TX, Marion Koogler McNay Art Museum, Nov. 3, 1998–Jan. 3, 1999.

New York, Solomon R. Guggenheim Museum, Gabriele Münter: Contours of a World, Nov. 7, 2025-Apr. 26, 2026, no cat. no.
data.provenance_text: The artist, Munich; sold to Arthur Jerome Eddy (1859-1920), Chicago, 1914 [Barnett 2022]; by descent to his wife Lucy O. Eddy (1863-1931) and son Jerome O. Eddy (1891-1951), Chicago, 1920; given to the Art Institute of Chicago, 1931.
data.copyright_notice: © 2018 Artists Rights Society (ARS), New York / VG Bild-Kunst, Bonn
data.on_loan_display: On loan to Solomon R. Guggenheim Museum in New York City for Gabriele Münter: Contours of a World

data.title: View of an Italian Villa and Gardens (the Belvedere of the Vatican)

thumbnail.alt_text: A work made of brush and gray wash heightened with white gouache, over traces of black chalk, on ivory laid paper.

data.medium_display: Brush and gray wash heightened with white gouache, over traces of black chalk, on ivory laid paper
data.credit_line: Gift of Mr. John L. Strauss
data.exhibition_history: Beijing, China, Palace Museum, "Rejoicing in Woods and Springs:  A Journey through Garden Cultures in China and the Wider World", April 1 - June 30, 2025, traveled to Hong Kong Museum, April 23 - July 29, 2026.
data.provenance_text: John L. Strauss, Glencoe, Ill., by 1959; given to the Art Institute, 1959.

input: View of an Italian Villa and Gardens (the Belvedere of the Vatican)
